title: Interfacial Reconstructions and Engineering in III-V@II-VI Core-Shell Quantum Dots
authors: Jordi Llusar, Abdessamad El Adel, Luca De Trizio, Liberato Manna, Zeger Hens, Ivan Infante
abstract: In core/shell quantum dots (QDs), the interface between semiconductors of different chemical character largely determines their optoelectronic properties. In III-V/II-VI systems, this boundary involves pronounced chemical and electronic discontinuities that can generate trap states even under complete surface passivation. Using density functional theory on atomistic models of InAs/CdSe QDs, we systematically reconstruct atomic arrangements at the surface and interface to evaluate how local coordination and interfacial dipoles influence the electronic structure. Abrupt interfaces induce charge imbalance and band-gap collapse, whereas introducing an alloyed interlayer that mixes core and shell atoms and vacancies restores energetic alignment and yields delocalized band-edge states, consistent with experimental findings. We also introduce a charge-flow analysis that quantifies charge redistribution across the QD, providing a framework for realistic modeling of interlayer formation and predictive design of defect-free interfaces in core@shell architectures.
summary: This theoretical study uses DFT to model InAs@CdSe quantum dots, demonstrating that the interface between different semiconductor materials is critical for optoelectronic performance. The authors show that abrupt interfaces create detrimental charge imbalances, but by modeling chemical reconstruction or alloying at the interface, they can restore the desired electronic band alignment, leading to defect-free quantum confinement.
main_problem: The study investigates how interfacial reconstructions and local atomic coordination dictate the electronic structure and trap state formation in III-V/II-VI core-shell quantum dots.
main_result: Abrupt core-shell interfaces lead to unphysical charge imbalances and band-gap collapse, whereas introducing an alloyed interlayer restores energetic alignment and yields delocalized, trap-free band-edge states.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations are employed on atomistic models, supplemented by charge-flow analysis to quantify charge redistribution across the QD interface.
model_or_system: The primary model system is the InAs@CdSe core-shell quantum dot, comparing idealized abrupt interfaces against reconstructed or alloyed interlayer structures.
key_observables: Electronic structure (HOMO-LUMO gap), charge-flow vectors, local coordination, and the spatial localization of electronic wavefunctions.
important_parameters: The chemical discontinuity between III-V and II-VI semiconductors, and the degree of atomic mixing/alloying at the interface.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the outer shell surface is reconstructed in all cases to isolate the core-shell interface effect, and that the charge-flow analysis enforces global charge neutrality.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate atomic structures, Projected Density of States (PDOS), and charge-flow maps comparing pure, abrupt, and reconstructed InAs@CdSe models, showing the recovery of the band gap upon reconstruction.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the problem of interfacial traps, then using DFT to model the electronic consequences of abrupt interfaces, and finally proposing and calculating the effects of chemically reconstructed/alloyed interlayers to achieve defect-free electronic states.
why_interesting: The detailed, atomistic modeling of interface chemistry and its direct link to electronic band structure is highly relevant for understanding engineered quantum emitters and open quantum systems where interface quality dictates coherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
